interpretation: SLITRK4 (SLIT and NTRK-like protein 4) is primarily involved in synaptogenesis and promotes synapse differentiation, which suggests a role in neural development and possibly in the response to injury. However, there is no direct evidence linking SLITRK4 to Steroid-Induced Osteonecrosis of the Femoral Head (SONFH) from the retrieved sources. The absence of specific studies connecting SLITRK4 to SONFH indicates that its relevance as a biomarker for early detection in this context is weak. The biological inference is that while SLITRK4 may play a role in cellular processes, its connection to SONFH remains speculative and unsubstantiated by current literature. Therefore, SLITRK4 is unlikely to serve as a clinically actionable biomarker for SONFH.
relevance_summary: SLITRK4 has no established connection to Steroid-Induced Osteonecrosis of the Femoral Head (SONFH) based on current evidence.

Disease: influenza A (H1N1)
Description: Viral infectious disease caused by the H1N1 strain of the influenza type A virus. Symptoms can range from mild to extremely severe.

result.title: SLITRK4 ↔ colorectal carcinoma
result.text:
Disease: colorectal carcinoma
Description: A malignant epithelial neoplasm that arises from the colon or rectum and invades through the muscularis mucosa into the submucosa. The vast majority are adenocarcinomas.
Association score: 0.075

result.title: SLITRK4 ↔ hepatocellular carcinoma
result.text:
Disease: hepatocellular carcinoma
Description: A malignant tumor that arises from hepatocytes. Hepatocellular carcinoma is relatively rare in the United States but very common in all African countries south of the Sahara and in Southeast Asia. Most cases are seen in patients over the age of 50 years, but this tumor can also occur in younger individuals and even in children. Hepatocellular carcinoma is more common in males than females and is associated with hepatitis B, hepatitis C, chronic alcohol abuse and cirrhosis. Serum elevation of alpha-fetoprotein occurs in a large percentage of patients with hepatocellular carcinoma. Grossly, hepatocellular carcinoma may present as a single mass, as multiple nodules, or as diffuse liver involvement. Microscopically, there is a wide range of differentiation from tumor to tumor (well differentiated to poorly differentiated tumors). Hepatocellular carcinomas quickly metastasize to regional lymph nodes and lung. The overall median survival of untreated liver cell carcinoma is about 4 months. The most effective treatment of hepatocellular carcinoma is complete resection of the tumor. Lately, an increasing number of tumors have been treated with liver transplantation.
Association score: 0.075

result.title: SLITRK4 ↔ gastric cancer
result.text:
Disease: gastric cancer
Description: A primary or metastatic malignant neoplasm involving the stomach.
Association score: 0.071
